statusLine: European fuel stocks are under strain with 19 of 27 countries below safety thresholds in at least one fuel type, though EU averages remain above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure across multiple countries, with Romania, Poland, and Luxembourg showing critical shortages across all three fuel types. Romania presents the most severe situation with petrol at just 40.4 days and diesel at 30.2 days—well below the 90-day mandatory minimum. Poland similarly struggles with petrol at 66.6 days and diesel at 74.7 days, while both countries face particularly acute jet fuel shortages at 76.4 days and 25.3 days respectively.

Jet fuel emerges as the most vulnerable fuel type, with 16 of 27 countries holding critical or warning-level reserves. This aviation fuel shortage affects major economies including Germany (72.8 days), France (67.5 days), and Spain (42.1 days). Despite these individual country challenges, EU-wide averages remain above the 90-day threshold: petrol at 112.8 days, diesel at 103.1 days, and jet fuel at 96.7 days, indicating that well-stocked countries like Estonia (292 days petrol) and Slovenia (365 days jet fuel) are offsetting shortages elsewhere.

The current situation reflects the impact of Middle East supply disruptions and strategic reserve releases, with Brent crude climbing 25.7% since late February to $91.12 per barrel. Fuel prices vary dramatically across the bloc, with Danish petrol reaching €2.50 per liter compared to Malta's €1.34, suggesting uneven supply conditions and varying national fuel policies across member states.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries show critical or warning status in at least one fuel type, with Romania, Poland, and Luxembourg facing critical shortages across all fuels
Jet fuel reserves are most vulnerable, with 16 countries below safe levels and major economies like Germany (72.8 days) and France (67.5 days) at critical status
Romania faces the most severe crisis with diesel at just 30.2 days and petrol at 40.4 days—far below the 90-day mandatory minimum
EU-wide fuel averages remain above safety thresholds due to well-stocked Nordic and Baltic states offsetting shortages in Central and Southern Europe
Brent crude has surged 25.7% since late February to $91.12/barrel, with fuel prices varying dramatically from €2.50/L in Denmark to €1.34/L in Malta
